title: Jupiter: King of the Gods
content: Jupiter, also known as Jove, is the sky god and king of the gods in Roman mythology. He is the Roman equivalent of the Greek god Zeus. As the patron deity of the Roman state, he was primarily concerned with the preservation of the Republic. His central temple was the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus on the Capitoline Hill. He is often associated with the eagle and the thunderbolt, symbolizing his sovereignty over both the heavens and the earthly laws of men. Extensive lore about this deity covers ritual practice, festival observance, iconography, literary reception, legal invocations, and political appropriation. title: Janus: The God of Transitions
content: Janus is the god of beginnings, gates, transitions, time, duality, and endings. Unlike many Roman gods, Janus has no direct Greek counterpart. He is famously depicted with two faces—one looking toward the past and the other toward the future. The month of January is named after him. In the Roman Forum, the gates of his temple were left open during times of war and were only closed during the rare periods of total peace throughout the empire. Extensive scholarly discussion covers Janus's role at thresholds, his calendrical importance, ritual uses at gates and doorways, and his symbolic presence at moments of political transition. title: Mars: War and Agriculture
content: Mars was the Roman god of war and an agricultural guardian. This dual nature reflected the early Roman lifestyle of the 'soldier-farmer.' He was the father of Romulus and Remus, making him a literal ancestor of the Roman people. While the Greek Ares was often seen as a destructive force, Mars was revered as a peaceful protector of the Roman state. The month of March (Martius) is named for him, marking the time when both military campaigns and the farming season began. Extensive material evidence and literary sources discuss Mars's agrarian festivals such as the Feriae Martiales, votive offerings, and iconography connecting the god to ploughing, cattle, and military standards. title: Minerva: Wisdom and Strategy
content: Minerva was the goddess of wisdom, justice, law, and strategic warfare. She was also the patroness of weaving, embroidery, and all skilled crafts. Along with Jupiter and Juno, she formed the Capitoline Triad, the most important deities in the Roman state religion. While Mars represented the physical brutality of war, Minerva represented the strategic and intellectual side of military conflict. She is frequently depicted with an owl, signifying her deep association with insight and education. Extensive discussion appears in sources on the Parthenon-style iconography adopted in Roman contexts, Minerva's guild-like patronage of craftspersons, and her legal and civic associations in Roman civic ritual. title: Vesta and the Sacred Fire
content: Vesta was the virgin goddess of the hearth, home, and family. She was rarely depicted in human form; instead, her presence was felt in the sacred fire kept burning in her circular temple in the Roman Forum. This fire was guarded by the Vestal Virgins, who were priestesses bound by a thirty-year vow of chastity. The fire of Vesta was considered the 'eternal flame' of Rome; if it ever went out, it was believed that a great disaster would strike the city. Academic treatments examine the Vestals' privileges, legal status, votive production, and the sociopolitical symbolism of an eternal hearth in civic religion. title: The Founding of Rome: Lupa and the Twins
content: The foundation myth of Rome begins with the twin brothers Romulus and Remus. Abandoned as infants by the banks of the Tiber River, they were discovered and suckled by a she-wolf, known as the Lupa. Eventually raised by a shepherd, the brothers returned to found a city on the spot where they were saved. After a fatal dispute over which hill to build on, Romulus killed Remus and founded the city on the Palatine Hill in 753 BC, naming it Rome after himself. The legend is treated as a foundational charter myth, and modern scholarship explores its use in identity construction, monumental program, and elite genealogy. title: Venus: Ancestry and Love
content: Venus was the goddess of love, beauty, fertility, and victory. In Roman mythology, she held a special status as the mother of the Roman hero Aeneas, who fled from Troy to Italy. This made her the divine ancestor of the Roman people and specifically the Julian clan, to which Julius Caesar belonged. Her influence extended beyond romantic love to the 'venustas' (charm and grace) required for social harmony and the success of the empire. Studies of her cult cover sanctuaries, civic imagery, poetic invocations, and her role in imperial propaganda connecting elite families to divine ancestry. title: Mercury: The Messenger of Commerce
content: Mercury was the god of financial gain, commerce, eloquence, and communication. He served as the messenger of the gods and the guide of souls to the underworld. Often depicted with winged sandals (talaria) and a winged hat (petasos), he carried the caduceus—a staff entwined with two snakes. Because of his association with trade and luck, his temple was located near the Circus Maximus, a central hub for Roman merchants and travelers. Archaeological and epigraphic materials reveal merchants' dedications to Mercury, his association with guilds, and the mobility-related rituals invoked by travelers.
